title: SpaceX Launches 81 Satellites in a Single Rideshare Mission, Its 79th Falcon 9 Flight of 2026
excerpt: SpaceX’s Transporter-17 rideshare mission delivered 81 separate payloads to a Sun-synchronous orbit in a single flight,…
body:
SpaceX’s Transporter-17 rideshare mission delivered 81 separate payloads to a Sun-synchronous orbit in a single flight, over a deployment window lasting roughly two and a half hours, according to Space.com.

What a “rideshare” mission is

Rather than launching a single large satellite, SpaceX’s Transporter series bundles dozens of small satellites from different customers — universities, startups, government agencies — onto one Falcon 9 flight, splitting the launch cost between everyone on board. It’s become one of the most cost-effective ways for smaller organizations to get a satellite into orbit at all.

A busy year for Falcon 9

This was the 79th Falcon 9 launch of 2026 alone. Nearly 80% of this year’s Falcon 9 missions have gone toward building out Starlink, SpaceX’s own broadband satellite constellation, which recently crossed roughly 10,839 active satellites in orbit — underscoring just how much of the current commercial launch market is now dominated by a single company’s own internal projects.

Why it matters

The cadence here is the real story: a single rocket family launching nearly 80 times in seven months is a launch tempo that simply didn’t exist a decade ago, and it’s what’s made both mega-constellations like Starlink and cheap rideshare access for small satellite operators possible at the same time.

Source: Space.com.
